metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's dry_run safety claim: `--dry-run` silently drops records missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` (no warning, no count increment in dry_run mode); update the Remediation and AC7 guidance to instruct operators to validate fallback payload structure before import (e.g., by inspecting `warnings` from a live `--dry-run` run against a scratch tracker, or by pre-validating fields), rather than relying solely on `skipped`/`would_create` counts.
evidence: import_ndjson.py lines 144-165: dry_run branch does `if not sid or not rec.get('ticket_type'): continue` with NO warn() call and NO skipped increment — malformed records are invisible to the operator.

import_ndjson.py lines 222-224: live path calls warn() but also does NOT increment `skipped` for missing-field records — the return summary's `skipped` count covers only already-imported source_ids, not field-missing drops.
Plan asserts: '--dry-run's skipped/would_create counts are the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload before it is dropped' — contradicted by the silent-continue in dry_run.
AC7 requires the guide to state records are 'silently skipped-with-a-logged-warning' — but in dry_run mode there IS no logged warning, making the AC7 characterization partially incorrect.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `--dry-run`'s `skipped`/`would_create` counts are 'the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload before it is dropped', but the actual dry_run code path (import_ndjson.py lines 148-151) silently `continue`s records missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` — it neither increments `skipped` nor calls `warn()`. The counts therefore give a false 'all clear' for a malformed payload: a payload missing `ticket_id` will show `would_create=0, skipped=0, warnings=[]`, indistinguishable from a correctly-processed run. The live path (lines 222-224) does call `warn()` but also does not increment `skipped`. This means the prescribed dry_run safety check in both the Remediation section and AC7 is unreliable for its stated purpose.
findings.location: Remediation section ('--dry-run's skipped/would_create counts are the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload') and AC7 ('correctly describes missing-ticket_id/ticket_type records as silently skipped-with-a-logged-warning... recommends rebar import --dry-run first to catch skips')
scenarios: An operator prepares a fallback NDJSON payload but accidentally omits `ticket_id`. Running `rebar import --dry-run` returns `{would_create: 0, skipped: 0, warnings: []}` with no indication of the drop. The operator concludes the payload is valid and proceeds; the live import silently drops every record (live path warns but does not fail). Zero tickets are created with no actionable error.

The guidance document (once written per AC7) instructs operators to trust dry_run counts as the safety net — a false premise baked in from the plan stage that will mislead every future operator following the prescribed workflow.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Remediation paragraph and AC7 verifiable text, replace 'dry-run's skipped/would_create counts are the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload' with: 'A record missing ticket_id or ticket_type is silently dropped in both dry-run and live mode (dry-run emits no warning; live mode emits a logged warning but does not increment skipped). Operators MUST validate that each fallback payload line carries both fields before invoking rebar import; rebar import --dry-run alone is NOT sufficient to detect missing required fields.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the dry-run detection guidance: state that operators should verify `would_create + skipped == record_count` (where record_count is the line count of the NDJSON file) after `--dry-run`, because records missing `ticket_id` or `ticket_type` are not reflected in either counter and only a count mismatch reveals them.
evidence: Plan: 'since a record missing ticket_id/ticket_type is silently skipped (with a logged warning) rather than rejected, --dry-run's skipped/would_create counts are the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload before it is dropped'
import_ndjson.py lines 144-156: `if not sid or not rec.get('ticket_type'): continue` — this `continue` fires before `skipped += 1` or `would += 1`, so neither counter reflects the dropped record
The dry_run branch only logs a warning in the non-dry-run path (line 224 in the live pass), not in the dry_run path — so --dry-run produces no warning and no count signal for the missing-field case
findings.finding: The plan's stated dry-run detection mechanism for malformed fallback payloads is mechanically incorrect: it claims '--dry-run's skipped/would_create counts are the only way to catch a record missing ticket_id/ticket_type before it is dropped,' but the actual import_ndjson.py dry_run path silently continues past such records WITHOUT incrementing either `would_create` or `skipped` (line 150-151: `if not sid or not rec.get('ticket_type'): continue` executes before the skipped/would increment). A malformed record disappears from both counters, so an operator comparing `input_count == would_create + skipped` would see a discrepancy — but the plan never tells the operator to perform this comparison. As written, the guidance trains operators to trust skipped/would_create as the catch, when those counts are silent on the failure case they're supposed to detect.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Exceptional import mechanism' subsection; AC7 verify step
scenarios: An agent generates a fallback payload with ticket_type misspelled or omitted; the operator runs rebar import --dry-run, sees would_create=0 and skipped=0 (for a 1-record file), infers the record is already imported, and proceeds without ingest — ticket is silently lost.
A maintainer auditing a bulk fallback NDJSON runs --dry-run and sees would_create + skipped == input_count - N with no explanation for the discrepancy, having no guidance on what to check.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the skipped/would_create counts are the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload' with: 'Run `rebar import --dry-run` and verify that `would_create + skipped` equals the number of lines in the NDJSON file; a discrepancy means one or more records were silently dropped for missing `ticket_id` or `ticket_type` (those records do not appear in either counter — check warnings output in the live path for details).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'Exceptional import mechanism' guidance to state that `rebar import --dry-run` does NOT surface missing-`ticket_id`/`ticket_type` records in its `skipped`/`would_create` counts — those records disappear silently in the dry-run path; the real catch mechanism is checking whether the live import's stderr warnings list is non-empty (or whether `warnings` in the returned metadata is non-empty), and update AC7 accordingly.
evidence: Plan states: '--dry-run's skipped/would_create counts are the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload before it is dropped'
import_ndjson.py dry_run path (lines 144-165): for records missing sid or ticket_type, code does `continue` with no counter increment and no warn() call
The warn() path (line 224) only fires in the live (non-dry-run) pass1 loop, not in the dry_run branch
Only records with valid ticket_id already in the store are counted as `skipped += 1` in dry_run
findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'rebar import --dry-run first [is the way] to catch a malformed fallback payload before it is dropped' and frames skipped/would_create counts as the detection signal. However, in the actual dry_run code path (import_ndjson.py lines 144-165), records missing ticket_id or ticket_type execute a bare `continue` with NO counter increment and NO warning emitted — they are invisible in both would_create and skipped. The warnings list (echoed to stderr) is the real signal, not the counts. The plan's guidance will teach future agents to trust dry-run counts as a malformed-record detector, but that mechanism does not work for the specific class of malformed record (missing required fields) the guidance is designed to catch.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Exceptional import mechanism' subsection; AC7
scenarios: An agent following the guidance runs rebar import --dry-run on a fallback payload where ticket_type is accidentally omitted. The dry-run reports skipped=0, would_create=0 with no warning, giving a false all-clear. The agent proceeds to run live import, where the record is silently dropped with only a stderr warning — which the agent may not observe.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the dry-run catch claim with: 'Run `rebar import --dry-run` and verify that `would_create` equals 1 (or the expected count) — a `would_create` of 0 with no warnings means the record's `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` were absent and it was silently skipped even in the dry-run path. On live import, inspect stderr for any warning lines beginning "skipping record without ticket_id/ticket_type"; only then run `rebar validate`/`rebar fsck`.' Also update AC7's verify clause to test that the guide names stderr warnings (not just counts) as the skip signal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the dry-run detection guidance: the `skipped` counter in dry-run mode counts already-imported records (by source_id), NOT malformed records; malformed records (missing ticket_id/ticket_type) produce no counter increment and no warning in the dry-run path — the actual detection mechanism is checking `warnings` in the returned metadata or inspecting stderr for logged warnings on a live (non-dry-run) small-scale trial, not inspecting `skipped`/`would_create` counts.
evidence: Plan states: 'since a record missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` is silently skipped (with a logged warning) rather than rejected, `--dry-run`'s `skipped`/`would_create` counts are the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload before it is dropped'

src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py lines 148-155 (dry_run branch): `sid = rec.get('ticket_id'); if not sid or not rec.get('ticket_type'): continue` — no counter incremented, no warn() called in dry-run path for this case.
The `skipped` counter on line 153 only increments when `sid in seen` (already-imported by source_id), not for missing-field records.
A payload with all records missing ticket_id would yield `{would_create: 0, skipped: 0}` — the plan's prescribed detection mechanism produces a false negative.
findings.finding: The plan claims '`--dry-run`'s `skipped`/`would_create` counts are the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload before it is dropped' — but this is mechanically incorrect per the actual dry-run code. In `import_ndjson.py` lines 148-155, the dry-run loop uses `continue` (not an increment) for records missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type`, so a completely malformed payload produces `skipped=0, would_create=0` — identical to a legitimately empty import. The `skipped` counter in dry-run mode only counts records whose `source_id` already exists in the target, NOT validation failures. The plan's guidance therefore teaches the executor to check a counter that gives no signal for the failure mode it is supposed to detect.
findings.location: Remediation / Exceptional import mechanism subsection; AC7
scenarios: Operator runs `rebar import --dry-run` on a malformed fallback payload (e.g., all records missing ticket_id). Sees `would_create: 0, skipped: 0`. Interprets this as 'nothing to import, payload already ingested' rather than 'all records are malformed and will be silently dropped on live import'. Proceeds with live import and loses all records silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the dry-run guidance with: 'Run `rebar import --dry-run <payload.ndjson>` and inspect the `warnings` list in the returned metadata (also echoed to stderr). A malformed fallback payload that skips records due to missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` will emit a warning per skipped line — `would_create: 0` alone is NOT sufficient to detect this (it also appears for an empty or already-imported payload). Confirm `would_create` equals the expected number of new tickets AND `warnings` is empty before proceeding with the live import.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the exceptional import mechanism guidance: the `--dry-run` path silently drops records missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` without incrementing `skipped` or emitting a warning, so operators must independently verify the fallback payload's `ticket_id` and `ticket_type` fields are present before running `rebar import --dry-run` (e.g., via `jq 'select(.ticket_id and .ticket_type)'`), and must check stderr after the live run — not rely solely on `would_create`/`skipped` counts to catch malformed payloads.
evidence: Plan text: 'a record missing ticket_id/ticket_type is silently skipped (with a logged warning) rather than rejected, --dry-run's skipped/would_create counts are the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload before it is dropped'
import_ndjson.py lines 144–165 (dry_run branch): records failing `if not sid or not rec.get('ticket_type'): continue` are silently discarded — no warn(), no skipped increment, no would increment
import_ndjson.py line 224 (live path): `warn(f'skipping record without ticket_id/ticket_type: ...')` IS called in the live path, but this code is not reached in the dry_run branch
Evidence class: Contradicted — the dry_run diagnostic value asserted by the plan is falsified by the implementation
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `rebar import --dry-run`'s `skipped`/`would_create` counts are 'the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload before it is dropped,' but the actual dry-run code path in `import_ndjson.py` (lines 144–165) silently discards records missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` without incrementing either counter and without emitting a warning — making the stated diagnostic mechanism inoperative for the exact failure mode it is supposed to catch.
findings.location: Remediation / Exceptional import mechanism section; AC7 text ('--dry-run's skipped/would_create counts are the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload')
scenarios: An operator prepares a fallback payload that omits `title` (not checked by importer) or has a malformed `ticket_id`; runs `--dry-run`; sees `would_create: 1, skipped: 0, warnings: []` — payload appears valid; runs live import; record is silently dropped with only a stderr warning that may go unnoticed
More critically: a payload missing `ticket_id` entirely gets `would_create: 0, skipped: 0` under --dry-run (invisible), giving false assurance before live import silently drops it
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Exceptional import mechanism' guidance, replace 'skipped/would_create counts are the only way to catch a malformed fallback payload' with: 'Before running `rebar import --dry-run`, validate that every line in the fallback payload has both `ticket_id` and `ticket_type` set (e.g., `jq -c 'select(.ticket_id and .ticket_type)' payload.ndjson | wc -l` should equal total line count). Under `--dry-run`, records missing those fields are silently discarded without warning and without affecting any counter; only the live run emits a stderr warning for dropped records. After the live run, inspect stderr for any "skipping record" warnings before concluding the import succeeded.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 6th invariant to `tests/unit/test_chatgpt_guide_links.py` asserting that `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` contains language describing the silent-skip behavior (e.g., matches a pattern like `silently skip` or `skipped.*warning` or `logged warning`) so the test fails if the guide claims records are rejected rather than dropped.
evidence: AC7 requires: 'correctly describes missing-ticket_id/ticket_type records as silently skipped-with-a-logged-warning (not schema-validation-rejected)'
AC8 invariant 4 checks only: 'The guide names rebar import (not a raw event-file write) as the only sanctioned exceptional ingest mechanism' — no check for silent-skip wording
AC8 invariant list (1-5) contains no entry for silent-skip/logged-warning language in the guide
ABSENCE: no invariant checks for 'silently skipped', 'logged warning', 'skipped count', or 'warning' in the guide
findings.finding: AC8's five test invariants have no assertion that the guide correctly describes the `rebar import` silent-skip behavior (missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` records are silently dropped with a logged warning, not schema-validation-rejected). AC7 explicitly requires the guide to state this, and it is the most safety-critical claim in the exceptional import path — an operator who doesn't know about silent skips will believe a malformed fallback payload was imported when it was actually dropped. The test plan closes without verifying that the guide communicates this failure path.
findings.location: AC8 (tests/unit/test_chatgpt_guide_links.py invariants 1-5) and AC7 (exceptional import mechanism requirements)
scenarios: A guide author writes that `rebar import` 'rejects' malformed records rather than 'silently skips' them — all 5 invariants pass green, but the guide contains a false and dangerous claim
An operator runs `rebar import` on a fallback payload missing `ticket_id`, sees no error, assumes the ticket was created, and the ticket is permanently lost with no alert
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to AC8's invariant list:
6. The guide describes missing-`ticket_id`/`ticket_type` records as silently skipped with a logged warning (not rejected): the guide contains a phrase matching `silently skip` or `skipped.*warn` or `logged warning` (case-insensitive) in the context of `rebar import`.

Implementation in the test:
```python
import re
guide = pathlib.Path('docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md').read_text()
assert re.search(r'silent(ly)?\s+skip|skipped.*warn|warn.*skipped|logged.{0,30}warn', guide, re.IGNORECASE), \
    'Guide must describe missing ticket_id/ticket_type records as silently skipped with a logged warning'
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a remediation step (or AC) to update `docs/cli-reference.md`'s `import` section to remove the stale 'always creates (re-running duplicates)' Note and replace it with accurate idempotency language, so the guide and the CLI reference are consistent.
evidence: cli-reference.md lines 220-222: 'Note: this importer always creates (re-running duplicates). Idempotent skip-by-source_id and deferred-push performance are added in a follow-up.'
import_ndjson.py lines 90-105 (_scan_existing_source_ids) and lines 118-142 (import_tickets docstring): idempotency IS implemented
import-export.md lines 67-69 correctly describes the idempotency as implemented
Plan relies on idempotency as a safety property for the fallback import path without flagging the cli-reference.md discrepancy
findings.finding: The plan's 'Exceptional import mechanism' asserts that `rebar import` idempotency is an established, currently-working behavior. This is confirmed by the code (import_ndjson.py). However, `docs/cli-reference.md` lines 220-222 contains a stale 'Note' that contradicts this: 'this importer always creates (re-running duplicates). Idempotent skip-by-source_id and deferred-push performance are added in a follow-up.' A future ChatGPT session consulting `cli-reference.md` to verify the idempotency guarantee will find conflicting authoritative documentation, potentially leading it to distrust the behavior the guide relies on. The plan does not flag or remediate this inconsistency.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Exceptional import mechanism' subsection; Evidence bullet citing docs/import-export.md:52-64
scenarios: A ChatGPT session reads cli-reference.md to verify operator steps before invoking rebar import on a fallback payload. It finds the 'always creates (re-running duplicates)' warning and either abandons the import path or runs it only once under uncertainty, not trusting the idempotency guarantee the guide's safe-retry model depends on.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `rebar import` is 'idempotent by source_id' throughout (Remediation section and AC7), but the pinned CLI --help text in docs/cli-reference.md explicitly contradicts this: 'Note: this importer always creates (re-running duplicates). Idempotent skip-by-source_id and deferred-push performance are added in a follow-up.' The implementation code and docs/import-export.md do show idempotency is implemented, but the CLI's authoritative --help surface says otherwise — a MISMATCH that means the plan's guidance (and the test at AC7) may be asserting behavior the installed binary's --help disavows.
findings.location: Remediation section ('Exceptional import mechanism'); AC7 ('is idempotent by source_id'); also docs/cli-reference.md:220-222 (the contradicting surface)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-task to regenerate `docs/cli-reference.md` (via `python scripts/gen_cli_reference.py`) after confirming the live binary's `rebar import --help` output has been updated to reflect the implemented idempotency. The plan should note: 'guidance is written against the implemented behavior in `import_ndjson.py`; the stale help text is a separate fix (run gen_cli_reference.py after updating the --help string in the import command).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verify-command check for AC7 that asserts the guide mentions the skip/warning behavior, e.g. `grep -qi 'silently skip\|skipped.*warn\|warn.*skip' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`.
evidence: AC7 intent: 'correctly describes missing-ticket_id/ticket_type records as silently skipped-with-a-logged-warning (not schema-validation-rejected)'
Verify command does not grep for 'skipped', 'silently', 'warning', or any token asserting the skip behavior
VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY #4 (fixture invalidity): grep for 'rebar import' passes whether or not the skip behavior is described
findings.finding: AC7's verify commands (`grep -qi 'rebar import'`, `grep -qi 'rebar validate\|rebar fsck'`, `grep -qi 'dry-run'`) do not verify that the guide correctly characterizes missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type` records as *silently skipped with a logged warning* (not schema-validation-rejected), which AC7 names as a required statement. A guide that says 'records failing validation are rejected' would pass all three greps while contradicting the actual behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC7 verify command
scenarios: An executor writes that 'rebar import rejects records missing ticket_id/ticket_type with a schema validation error' — a plausible but wrong description — and still passes all three AC7 greps.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC7's verify step to include grep patterns covering the silent-skip behavior (e.g., `grep -qi 'silently skip\|logged warning' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`), the NDJSON-not-raw-events distinction (e.g., `grep -qi 'ndjson' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`), and the prohibition on raw event-file injection (e.g., `grep -qi 'raw event' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`).
evidence: AC7 verify: 'grep -qi \'rebar import\' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi \'rebar validate\\|rebar fsck\' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi \'dry-run\' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md'

AC7 text requires the guide to 'correctly describes missing-`ticket_id`/`ticket_type` records as silently skipped-with-a-logged-warning (not schema-validation-rejected)' — no grep for 'silently skipped' or 'logged warning' appears in the verify step
AC7 text requires 'never raw event-file injection or Contents API writes' — no grep for 'event-file' or 'contents api' in AC7's verify step
findings.finding: AC7's verify step checks only three grep patterns (`rebar import`, `rebar validate|rebar fsck`, `dry-run`) but the AC text specifies approximately seven distinct requirements for the documented import mechanism: (1) names `rebar import`/`rebar.import_tickets()` as only sanctioned path, (2) never raw event-file injection or Contents API writes, (3) accepts NDJSON not raw events, (4) assigns fresh local ids/HLC timestamps, (5) correctly describes silently-skipped-with-a-logged-warning behavior for missing `ticket_id`/`ticket_type`, (6) recommends `--dry-run` first, (7) requires confirming target branch head, (8) requires `rebar validate`/`rebar fsck` afterward. Items (2), (3), (4), and (5) have no verify step coverage, so an implementation could omit them and still pass AC7's verify.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7 verify step
scenarios: An implementer writes a guide that describes `rebar import` and `rebar validate` but omits the silent-skip behavior warning and the NDJSON-not-raw-events distinction; AC7's verify step passes but the documented behavior is incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC7 verify step: `&& grep -qi 'silently skip' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'ndjson' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'raw event\|event.file' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`

findings.finding: AC7's grep proving command checks that 'rebar import' and 'rebar validate'/'rebar fsck' appear, but does not verify the other required statements: that the path accepts NDJSON (not raw events), assigns fresh local ids/HLC timestamps, requires confirming the target branch head before invocation, and prohibits raw event-file injection and Contents API writes.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in AC8(5) the required representation format for the fallback-payload field list in the guide (e.g., 'fields must appear in a fenced NDJSON or JSON code block listing all five required fields') so `test_chatgpt_guide_links.py` can parse and compare deterministically.
evidence: AC8(5): 'The guide's fallback-payload field list matches src/rebar/schemas/export.schema.json's actual required array (schema_version, ticket_id, ticket_type, title, status)'
No statement in the plan specifying how the field list is formatted in the guide document
Without a specified format, the test implementation is ambiguous: a regex over prose vs. a JSON block vs. a YAML block all yield different test logic
findings.finding: AC8(5) requires the test to assert 'the guide's fallback-payload field list matches `src/rebar/schemas/export.schema.json`'s actual `required` array', but neither AC8 nor the Remediation section specifies how the field list is represented in the guide (e.g., a fenced JSON block, a markdown table, a bullet list). An executor writing `test_chatgpt_guide_links.py` must invent the representation format, and a later reviewer of the guide might use a different format, breaking the test or making it trivially pass via substring search.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC8, invariant 5
scenarios: Executor A represents the fallback fields as a fenced JSON example and writes a test that parses JSON; Executor B represents them as a bullet list and uses a grep — the two implementations would agree or disagree on compliance depending on representation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC8(5): 'The guide must present the fallback payload as a fenced code block (json or ndjson) containing all five required fields; the test loads `src/rebar/schemas/export.schema.json`, extracts `required`, and asserts each field name appears in that fenced block.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the closed-set `ticket_type` enum values to the fallback payload schema specification: `bug`, `epic`, `story`, `task` (and note that `session_log`, `code_review`, `identity` are system types not appropriate for agent-created work tickets).
evidence: Plan: 'ticket_type/title/description from the ticket content' — no enum given
src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json lines 11-14: ticket_type enum is ['bug','epic','story','task','session_log','code_review','identity']
import_ndjson.py line 150: `if not sid or not rec.get('ticket_type'): continue` — a falsy or missing ticket_type silently skips the record

An unrecognized ticket_type string is truthy and passes the skip guard, but rebar create would then reject it — however the fallback payload is for offline review, so the only import-time gate is the truthy check
findings.finding: The fallback payload specification requires agents to populate `ticket_type` but never enumerates the legal enum values. The import silently skips any record where `ticket_type` is falsy (import_ndjson.py line 150), and `rebar create` would reject an unrecognized type. An agent instructed only that `ticket_type` comes 'from the ticket content' has no closed set to choose from and may produce an invalid value (e.g. 'feature', 'enhancement', 'chore') that is silently dropped by import — exactly the scenario the fallback payload design is trying to prevent.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Fallback payload schema' subsection; AC6
scenarios: A ChatGPT session creates a fallback payload with ticket_type='feature'; on later rebar import, the record is NOT silently skipped (truthy) but rebar create rejects the invalid type — the import emits a warning and the ticket is never created, with no guidance for the operator on how to fix it.
A session uses ticket_type='task' (correct) but another uses 'Task' (capital) — which is truthy and passes the skip guard but may fail create_ticket depending on normalization.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Fallback payload schema' subsection, add: 'ticket_type: one of `task`, `story`, `epic`, or `bug` (the work-ticket types; do not use system types `session_log`, `code_review`, or `identity`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the closed-set `ticket_type` enum values (`bug`, `epic`, `story`, `task`, `session_log`, `code_review`, `identity`, sourced from `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json`) to the fallback payload schema specification in the guidance document, so an agent can choose a valid value without consulting external files.
evidence: AC6 states: 'ticket_type/title/description from the ticket content' — but gives no enumeration of which ticket_type values are legal.

src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json line 11-13: `"ticket_type": { "type": "string", "enum": ["bug", "epic", "story", "task", "session_log", "code_review", "identity"] }` — this enum is never quoted or referenced in the plan.
ABSENCE: no plan section lists valid ticket_type values for the agent to choose from when composing a fallback payload.
findings.finding: The fallback payload schema requires the agent to supply a valid `ticket_type` value, but the plan never enumerates the closed-set enum values legal for that field. The actual enum — `["bug", "epic", "story", "task", "session_log", "code_review", "identity"]` — lives only in `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json` and is never cited or reproduced in the Remediation section, AC6, or anywhere the guidance document content is specified. A ChatGPT agent authoring a fallback payload has no authoritative list of valid types to choose from.
findings.location: Remediation / Fallback payload schema subsection; AC6
scenarios: A ChatGPT agent producing a fallback payload writes `ticket_type: 'feature'` or `ticket_type: 'improvement'` (plausible LLM hallucinations) — the record passes the plan's conformance check but silently fails or misbehaves on `rebar import`.
An agent unsure whether 'bug' vs 'task' is appropriate has no authoritative enum to consult from the guidance document alone.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Remediation 'Fallback payload schema' subsection and in AC6, add: 'Valid `ticket_type` values (from `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json`): `bug`, `epic`, `story`, `task`, `session_log`, `code_review`, `identity`.' The documentation test (AC8) should additionally assert no `ticket_type` value appears in the guide that is absent from the schema's enum.

evidence: 5 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: etc.
verify command `grep -qi 'chatgpt' AGENTS.md`.` greps the unanchored word 'chatgpt' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_chatgpt'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"chatgpt":')
verify command `grep -qi 'chatgpt' AGENTS.md` greps the unanchored word 'chatgpt' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_chatgpt'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"chatgpt":')

verify command `grep -qi 'tracker worktree' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'gerrit' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`.` greps the unanchored word 'tracker' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_tracker'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"tracker":')

verify command `grep -qi 'tracker worktree' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'gerrit' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` greps the unanchored word 'tracker' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_tracker'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"tracker":')

verify command `grep -qi 'read-only mirror' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable

verify command `grep -qi 'search-before-create' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable

verify command `grep -qi 'append-only' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`.` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable

verify command `grep -qi 'append-only' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable

verify command `grep -qi 'export.schema.json' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'schema_version' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`.` greps the unanchored word 'schema_version' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_schema_version'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"schema_version":')

verify command `grep -qi 'export.schema.json' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'schema_version' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` greps the unanchored word 'schema_version' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_schema_version'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"schema_version":')
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC2's verify command to also grep for the four omitted dimensions (local checkout, native binary, network/remote, GitHub connector), e.g. add `&& grep -qi 'local checkout\|native.*rebar' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'network\|remote access' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'github connector' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`.
evidence: AC2 intent: 'Guidance includes an environment capability check covering local checkout, native rebar/rebar-mcp, tracker worktree, network/remote access, GitHub connector, and Gerrit availability.'
Verify command: `grep -qi 'tracker worktree' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'gerrit' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`
Four dimensions (local checkout, native rebar/rebar-mcp, network/remote access, GitHub connector) have no corresponding grep
findings.finding: AC2's verify command checks only 2 of the 6 required capability-check dimensions ('tracker worktree' and 'gerrit'); the other four — local checkout, native `rebar`/`rebar-mcp` binary, network/remote access, and GitHub connector — are not verified. A guide that names only 'tracker worktree' and 'gerrit' while omitting the other four dimensions passes the grep but does not satisfy AC2's stated intent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2 verify command
scenarios: An executor writes a guide that mentions 'tracker worktree' and 'gerrit' but omits the native binary check and the GitHub connector check; the AC passes but the capability-check section is incomplete per the spec.

findings.finding: AC2's grep proving command checks only two of the six required capability dimensions ('tracker worktree' and 'gerrit'), leaving local checkout, native rebar/rebar-mcp binary, network/remote access, and GitHub connector unverified by any proving command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Verify: grep -qi 'tracker worktree' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'gerrit' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'local checkout' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'github connector' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'rebar-mcp' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'network' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC1's verify command to assert AGENTS.md contains a Markdown link pointing to `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`, e.g. `grep -qiE '\[.*\]\(.*docs/chatgpt-agent-guide\.md\)' AGENTS.md`.
evidence: "Verify: `grep -qi 'chatgpt' AGENTS.md`"
AC intent: 'discoverable from AGENTS.md … Verify: grep -qi chatgpt AGENTS.md' — discoverability requires a link, not merely the word 'chatgpt'
VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY #1 (substring false-positive): the pattern is not anchored to a link syntax
findings.finding: AC1's verify command (`grep -qi 'chatgpt' AGENTS.md`) proves only that the string 'chatgpt' appears somewhere in AGENTS.md, not that AGENTS.md contains an actual link to `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`. A comment such as '# ChatGPT sessions should see AGENTS.md' would satisfy the grep while making the guide undiscoverable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 verify command
scenarios: An executor adds a sentence 'Note: ChatGPT agents should read AGENTS.md' to AGENTS.md without adding a link, the grep passes, but the guide is never referenced by path and therefore not discoverable by a ChatGPT session scanning AGENTS.md for pointers.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the verify command with: `grep -qE '\(docs/chatgpt-agent-guide\.md\)' AGENTS.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a failure fallback to the exceptional import mechanism: specify that (a) a failed `rebar import` can be safely re-run due to idempotency-by-source_id, and (b) if `rebar validate`/`rebar fsck` reports errors after import, the operator should run `rebar fsck --repair-snapshots` for SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT findings and escalate other findings before treating the import as complete.
evidence: Plan states: 'the operator confirms the target branch is current (`git fetch` + `rebar exists <known-id>`) and runs `rebar import --dry-run` first … afterward, the operator runs `rebar validate` (or `rebar fsck`) against the tracker to confirm store integrity.'
Plan states `rebar import` is 'idempotent by source_id' (re-running is a no-op for already-ingested records) — this partially addresses partial-failure recovery for the import itself.
ABSENCE: no plan section specifies what the executor should do if `rebar validate`/`rebar fsck` reports errors after import, or if `rebar import` exits non-zero mid-run.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no fallback for the case where `rebar import` partially fails mid-run (e.g., a lock acquisition failure, a push failure, or a crash leaving a partial import) or where `rebar validate`/`rebar fsck` reports integrity errors after the import completes. The guidance prescribes these steps sequentially but leaves the executor with no stated recovery action when they fail.
findings.location: Remediation / Exceptional import mechanism subsection; AC7
scenarios: After `rebar import` completes, `rebar validate` reports a SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT finding. The guidance prescribes running validate but not what to do with its output — the executor is stuck.
rebar import fails mid-run (lock contention, exit 10). The operator re-runs — idempotency saves already-created records, but the executor has no guidance confirming this is safe.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a structural requirement to the plan specifying that `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` must have named top-level sections (e.g., Environment Capability Check, Decision Rule, Fallback Payload, Exceptional Import Path, Prohibitions) and, if the document exceeds ~150 lines, a navigable table of contents — so that each AC's content area maps to a findable heading.
evidence: AC2–AC7 each specify distinct content areas (capability check, read-only mirror, search-before-create, append-only, fallback schema, import mechanism) that together constitute a multi-section document
Remediation lists four distinct paragraphs of required content with no guidance on how they should be organized in the output file
No AC or Remediation line requires a table of contents, named headings, or section order for docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md
findings.finding: The plan mandates substantial content for `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` (6-dimension capability check, decision rule with named conditions, fallback payload schema with per-field values, exceptional import mechanism with multiple operational steps, prohibitions, and rationale) but imposes no structural requirements on the document — no required headings, sections, or table of contents. T7(c) requires large docs to have navigable structure; without it, a complex guidance document could be delivered as an undifferentiated wall of prose that satisfies every grep-based AC yet is unusable in practice.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all 8 ACs) / Remediation section — no structural requirement specified anywhere
scenarios: An implementer writes a single long prose section that passes all grep-based ACs but buries the decision rule inside a paragraph, making it hard for a future ChatGPT session to locate quickly
A maintainer updating one section (e.g., changing the fallback schema fields) cannot navigate to it without reading the whole document
findings.suggested_fix: In the Acceptance Criteria or Remediation, add a line such as: 'The guidance document MUST be organized with named headings matching at least: (1) Environment Capability Check, (2) Native-Creation Decision Rule, (3) Fallback Payload Schema, (4) Exceptional Import Path (`rebar import`), (5) Prohibited Operations. A table of contents is required if the document exceeds 150 lines.' This ensures the document is navigable without adding unnecessary complexity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note in docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md (or a co-located comment in the test) directing maintainers to update all prose descriptions of the fallback-payload field list when export.schema.json's required array changes, OR extend the AC8 test to scan for the field names in the guide's prose as well as the dedicated field-list section, so schema drift is caught holistically.
evidence: AC6 specifies: 'producing a reviewable ticket payload conforming to src/rebar/schemas/export.schema.json's required fields (schema_version: 1, an agent-generated UUID4 ticket_id, ticket_type, title, status: "open"...'
AC8 assertion #5: 'The guide's fallback-payload field list matches src/rebar/schemas/export.schema.json's actual required array'

The test validates the guide's field list against the schema file, but prose descriptions of the fields in the guide body are not covered by this assertion — if the schema gains a new required field, the test passes while the guidance prose is stale
findings.finding: The guidance doc hardcodes the export.schema.json required-field list (schema_version, ticket_id, ticket_type, title, status) as prose, but the plan establishes no mechanism to keep the guidance text in sync when the schema evolves — the AC8 test catches drift for the test's own assertion, but does not enforce that the guidance prose itself is updated, leaving the two representations able to diverge silently after a schema change.
findings.location: AC6, AC8 (assertion #5), Remediation — Fallback payload schema section
scenarios: A maintainer adds a required field to export.schema.json; AC8's test assertion #5 catches the mismatch in the guide's field list section, but explanatory prose elsewhere in the guide (e.g., in the fallback procedure steps) still names the old field set and silently misleads future agents.
Schema required array shrinks; guidance prose still instructs agents to populate a field that is no longer required, causing unnecessary confusion.
findings.suggested_fix: In test_chatgpt_guide_links.py, after reading the schema's required array, also assert that each required field name appears at least once in the guide's prose (case-insensitive substring match) — this makes any future schema-required-array change that isn't reflected anywhere in the guide a test failure, not a silent documentation gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for the documentation approach to the Remediation section — e.g., explaining why a separate `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` (not an AGENTS.md expansion) is chosen: AGENTS.md's lean-by-design constraint (startup context) makes a pointer-into-docs/ the appropriate pattern, and no codebase changes are needed because the gap is guidance, not functionality.
evidence: AGENTS.md explicitly states 'Keep this file lean: an @-import loads at launch and does not reduce context, so already-documented topics are one-line pointers into docs/' — this is the only implicit rationale for a separate doc, but it is not stated in the plan as a positive rationale for the chosen approach
The plan contains no sentence of the form 'We choose a separate guidance doc because...' or 'A documentation approach fits better than X because...'
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for choosing the documentation-only approach (a new docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md + lightweight test) over other structural alternatives. The Remediation section states what to build but not why this decomposition — rather than, say, a ChatGPT-specific section in AGENTS.md, or a runtime MCP capability-check tool — is the best fit for this codebase and problem.
findings.location: Remediation section (entire); Finding/Evidence sections
scenarios: An implementer who wants to simplify by embedding in AGENTS.md has no plan-stated reason not to, and may do so, bloating the startup context.
A future reviewer cannot evaluate whether the approach was deliberately chosen or defaulted.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Remediation section: 'A separate guidance doc (not an AGENTS.md expansion) is chosen because AGENTS.md is intentionally lean — it loads at launch — and the existing pointer-into-docs/ pattern handles ChatGPT-specific depth without burdening every other agent harness's startup context; no rebar binary or MCP changes are needed because the gap is guidance, not a missing capability.'

findings.finding: AC8's 'does not contradict canonical Rebar or Gerrit rules' clause is an unspecified design decision left to the executor: no step, no list of specific checks, and no concrete assertions describe what the test must contain to satisfy it. The proving command ('exits 0') exercises whatever the executor happens to write, not a defined set of invariants. This simultaneously violates E6(c/d) (no described step reaches the 'no-contradiction' end-state) and F1(a) (the observable outcome — what specific contradictions are detected — is not stated).
findings.location: AC8 / Acceptance Criteria, final bullet
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the vague 'does not contradict' clause with an explicit list of negative assertions the test must make, for example:
1. `assert 'github issues' not in guide_lower or 'not a substitute' in guide_lower` (GitHub Issues forbidden as tracker).
2. `assert 'contents api' not in guide_lower or 'not sanctioned' in guide_lower` (Contents-API write forbidden).
3. `assert re.search(r'\[.*chatgpt.*\]\(docs/chatgpt-agent-guide\.md\)', agents_md_text, re.I)` (link is a proper Markdown hyperlink, not just the word 'chatgpt').
State these assertion targets in the AC so the test is deterministically implementable and the proving command is meaningful.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that a fallback payload omitting any of the schema's required fields 'fails rebar import's schema validation' — but the actual importer (import_ndjson.py) does NOT invoke JSON Schema validation against export.schema.json; it silently skips records missing ticket_id or ticket_type (line ~150: `if not sid or not rec.get('ticket_type'): continue`), rather than raising a schema-validation error. This overstates the enforcement guarantee, which could mislead guidance consumers into expecting a hard failure when an incomplete fallback payload is fed to rebar import.
findings.location: Evidence section: 'a fallback payload that omits any of these fails rebar import's schema validation' and Remediation / Fallback payload schema section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'fails rebar import's schema validation' with: 'records missing ticket_id or ticket_type are silently skipped (counted in the skipped total); run rebar import --dry-run first and confirm would_create > 0 and skipped == 0 before the live import.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add verify-command lines for AC6 that check for the decision rule and UUID4 mechanic, e.g. `grep -qi 'uuid4\|uuid 4' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'source_id' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'tracker.worktree.*network\|network.*tracker.worktree' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` (or move these checks into AC8 as additional invariants).
evidence: AC6 intent: 'Guidance defines precisely when native ticket creation counts as impossible (the tracker-worktree-and-network/binary decision rule above) … an agent-generated UUID4 ticket_id … plus a transparent capability-gap report'
Verify command: `grep -qi 'export.schema.json' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md && grep -qi 'schema_version' docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md`
VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY #4 (fixture invalidity): greps that match incidental strings do not exercise the decision rule or UUID4 provenance claims
findings.finding: AC6's verify command (`grep -qi 'export.schema.json' … && grep -qi 'schema_version' …`) does not check that the guide states the precise impossibility-trigger decision rule (tracker-worktree-AND-network/binary failure) or that `ticket_id` is explicitly described as an agent-generated UUID4 that becomes `source_id` on import. Both are material requirements of AC6 that the grep cannot distinguish from a passing mention of these strings.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC6 verify command
scenarios: An executor documents the fallback payload fields but omits the two-part impossibility condition (tracker-worktree failure AND network/binary failure) and omits the UUID4/source_id mechanic; all greps still pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC8 test #3's implementation strategy: either (a) specify that the test checks only that the phrase 'contents api' does NOT appear in any positively-framed how-to section (e.g., after a heading not containing 'avoid'/'never'/'do not'), or (b) simplify to asserting that no sentence contains both 'contents api' and 'tickets branch' without a negation word ('never', 'not', 'avoid', 'prohibited') within the same sentence.
evidence: AC8 test #3: 'The guide does not describe a direct GitHub Contents-API write to the `tickets` branch as a sanctioned or normal path (no phrase pairing "contents api" with "tickets branch" in an affirmative/how-to context).'
ABSENCE: no explanation of how the unit test should distinguish 'affirmative/how-to context' from a cautionary prohibition that also pairs 'contents api' with 'tickets branch'
findings.finding: AC8 test #3 requires the automated unit test to detect that the guide does not describe a Contents-API write to the `tickets` branch 'in an affirmative/how-to context' — but a standard Python unit test cannot reliably distinguish affirmative from prohibitive/cautionary usage of the same phrase pair without NLP or manual inspection. The test spec does not resolve how to implement 'affirmative/how-to context' detection programmatically, leaving the criterion under-specified for the implementer.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC8, test invariant #3
scenarios: The plan's own guide text must say 'never use the Contents API to write to the tickets branch' — which pairs both terms in a prohibitive context. A naive co-occurrence grep would flag this prohibition as a violation of test #3, causing the test to fail incorrectly.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the 'affirmative/how-to context' qualifier with a concrete implementable rule, e.g.: 'no sentence in the guide contains both the substring "contents api" and the substring "tickets branch" unless the sentence also contains at least one of the words: never, not, avoid, prohibited, do not.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add guidance specifying that the agent must output the generated UUID4 prominently so the operator can reuse it if the fallback payload needs to be regenerated, and that an operator regenerating a payload for the same logical ticket MUST reuse the original UUID4 (not generate a fresh one) to preserve import idempotency.
evidence: 'ticket_id: <agent-generated UUID4, becomes source_id on import>' — the UUID4 is generated per payload, not per logical ticket

're-running the same fallback payload through rebar import later is then a no-op, not a duplicate' — idempotency only holds for the *same* payload bytes, not for independently generated payloads for the same logical ticket
No guidance is given on storing or reusing the generated UUID4 across session restarts or operator retries
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new behavioral contract — agent-generated UUID4 as the provenance id that becomes source_id on rebar import — but does not specify what happens if the same fallback session produces two separate payloads for the same logical ticket (e.g., a retry or copy-paste duplication by the operator): the two payloads will have different UUID4s and therefore both will be imported as distinct tickets rather than de-duplicated, silently creating duplicates.
findings.location: Remediation — Fallback payload schema section; AC6; AC7
scenarios: An operator loses the original fallback payload output and asks the agent to regenerate it; the agent generates a new UUID4; both are later imported, creating two tickets for the same work item.
An agent session is interrupted mid-output and the operator re-runs the same prompt; two payloads with different UUID4s are produced for the same ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: In the fallback procedure, add: 'Record the generated UUID4 immediately — if you must regenerate this payload (e.g., after a session restart), reuse the same UUID4 so that rebar import remains idempotent for this ticket. Do not generate a new UUID4 for a ticket you have already produced a fallback payload for.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the insertion point for the ChatGPT discoverability link in `AGENTS.md` (e.g., a new bullet in the 'Where to read' section, which is safely past the `test_codex_agent_guidance.py` landmark range), and enumerate `tests/unit/test_codex_agent_guidance.py` as a consumer to verify it still passes after the change.
evidence: tests/unit/test_codex_agent_guidance.py:10-11: `start = text.index('**Codex Gerrit workflow rule:')` / `end = text.index('\n\n## Record your work', start)` — parses a specific slice of AGENTS.md
Plan requires adding ChatGPT discoverability to AGENTS.md (AC1) but specifies no insertion point
Plan does not enumerate test_codex_agent_guidance.py as a consumer to verify
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_codex_agent_guidance.py` is an unenumerated consumer of `AGENTS.md` that parses the file between structural landmarks (`**Codex Gerrit workflow rule:**` … `\n\n## Record your work`). The plan modifies `AGENTS.md` to add a discoverable link to `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` but does not specify the insertion point, leaving open the risk that placement within or adjacent to the landmark range causes `text.index()` in the test to mismatch or the extracted slice to include unexpected content.
findings.location: AC1 ('A ChatGPT-specific guidance file or clearly labeled section is discoverable from AGENTS.md') / tests/unit/test_codex_agent_guidance.py
scenarios: Executor inserts the ChatGPT link between the Codex Gerrit rule block and the 'Record your work' heading; test_codex_agent_guidance.py extracts unexpected content in the guidance slice and fails on one of its eight string assertions.
findings.suggested_fix: Place the ChatGPT guide reference as a new bullet in AGENTS.md's '## Where to read' section (after the existing MCP tool set bullet), which is well past the `## Record your work` landmark that bounds the Codex guidance test's slice.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` to `docs/README.md` (under the Agent audience section) so the `scripts/check_docs_index.py --check` CI gate continues to pass.
evidence: scripts/check_docs_index.py:find_unindexed() reports every docs/*.md not linked from docs/README.md
`.github/workflows/_build-and-test.yml:330`: `python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check`
tests/unit/test_check_docs_index.py:44: `test_current_repo_index_is_clean` asserts the real docs/README.md passes both checks
Plan names `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` as the deliverable but contains no mention of `docs/README.md`
findings.finding: The plan creates `docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md` but never mentions updating `docs/README.md` to link to it. The CI 'Docs-index completeness gate' (`scripts/check_docs_index.py --check`, enforced in `.github/workflows/_build-and-test.yml:330`) will fail for any top-level `docs/*.md` not linked from `docs/README.md` — causing the Verified gate to reject the change.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1 / Remediation section (docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md creation)
scenarios: Executor creates docs/chatgpt-agent-guide.md without updating docs/README.md; CI Docs-index gate fails with 'unindexed doc: chatgpt-agent-guide.md'; Gerrit Verified vote is -1.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet under the 'Agent' audience section of docs/README.md, e.g.: `- **ChatGPT connector-limited environments** — capability check, fallback payload, and exceptional import path → [chatgpt-agent-guide.md](chatgpt-agent-guide.md).`

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
